ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic pilaf entry (FOODON_00002694); no specific okra pulao entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers rice dishes broadly; no specific Indian okra pulao code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code exists for okra pulao or Indian rice pilaf dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of ladies finger pulao is approximately 300 g, equivalent to roughly one heaped plate of rice with okra and spices.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable Pulao / Rice Pilaf with Vegetables — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entries for basmati rice and okra
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry for ladies finger pulao exists. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for cooked basmati rice (plain pulao base) and okra (bhindi) in proportions consistent with a standard recipe (~65% cooked basmati rice, ~20% okra, ~15% oil/spice/onion/tomato). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked long-grain white rice (NDB 20444) and cooked okra (NDB 11279) were used to cross-validate macros. Energy calculated as 4×protein + 4×carbohydrate + 9×fat.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — basmati rice cooked (analogous base); Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ladies finger pulao. GI estimated at 57 based on cooked basmati rice GI of 57–58 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021, Diabetes Care). Okra is a low-GI vegetable and its inclusion with fat and fibre is expected to moderately lower the overall GI; however, rice dominates the carbohydrate fraction (~65% by weight), so the dish GI is anchored to basmati rice GI. GL per serving = (57 × 25.2 × 300) / (100 × 100) = 43.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g serving provides approximately 630 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by basmati rice carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 57 and a glycemic load of approximately 43 per standard serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, pair with a protein source, and choose this dish over higher-GI rice preparations; the okra content contributes soluble fibre that may attenuate postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content with favourable unsaturated fat profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ladies finger pulao prepared with minimal ghee or vegetable oil provides a relatively low saturated fat load (~1.2 g per 100 g); the okra contributes soluble fibre (pectin) which may support LDL cholesterol reduction, making this a heart-friendly choice when portion-controlled.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ladies Finger Pulao (also known as Bhindi Pulao or Okra Pulao) is an aromatic Indian rice pilaf dish prepared by cooking basmati rice together with okra (ladies finger / bhindi) and a fragrant blend of whole spices including cumin, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaf. The dish is typically started by tempering whole spices in ghee or oil, followed by sautéing onions, ginger, garlic, and green chilli until golden, then adding sliced okra and tomatoes before incorporating washed basmati rice and water or stock to cook by absorption. The result is a fluffy, lightly spiced rice dish in which the okra softens and imparts a mild earthy flavour and slight viscosity. The texture is tender with distinct rice grains and soft okra pieces. Nutritionally, a 300 g serving provides approximately 444 kcal, 9.3 g protein, 11.4 g fat, and 75.6 g carbohydrate, with meaningful dietary fibre from the okra (~5.4 g per serving). The medium glycemic index (GI ~57) is anchored by basmati rice but partially moderated by okra's soluble fibre content. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its carbohydrate load, moderate concern for hypertension due to added salt, and low concern for cardiovascular disease given its unsaturated fat profile and fibre contribution. It is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements. In Singapore, this dish is served at Indian and biryani restaurants and is a popular lunch or dinner option.
common_user_queries: Is ladies finger pulao good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN or HPB entry for ladies finger pulao exists. Nutrient values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN cooked basmati rice and okra data in standard recipe proportions, cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy. GI is estimated from basmati rice GI in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the adapted/estimated nature of the nutrient and GI data.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy data for component ingredients (cooked basmati rice ~65%, okra ~20%, oil/spice/onion/tomato ~15%) as no direct analytical entry for ladies finger pulao exists in approved databases. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing B12; confirmed zero by ingredient composition. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA source entries for the component ingredients at the level of a mixed dish. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish type. 6. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate anchored to cooked basmati rice per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care). 7. The food_name has been normalised from 'Safit Ladies finger Pulao' (restaurant menu name) to the canonical English form 'Ladies Finger Pulao'; 'Safit' is a restaurant-specific descriptor and not part of the canonical dish name. 8. Serving size of 300 g is estimated based on typical Indian restaurant rice dish portions.
